en.extract:
The crested coua (Coua cristata) is a common medium-sized bird member of the cuckoo family, Cuculidae. It is endemic to Madagascar, mainly found in the coastal regions of the island. The crested coua is a weak flyer, so it will often be observed hopping from branch to branch in high canopies. It is distinguished mainly by its greenish-grey back, its prominent grey head crest, rufous-coloured breast, white abdomen and bright turquoise and blue patches of bare skin around the eyes.  


Description


Adults
The adult crested coua is a medium-sized bird, averaging between 12 and 17 inches (30 to 44 cm) in length. It has a greenish-gray plumage on the back, a white abdomen, rufous-colored chest and brown eyes. Its long tail is purplish-blue with white tips and its bill and beak are black. Its defining characteristics are its prominent grey head crest and, as goes for all couas, its colourful patches of bare skin around the eyes, which is blue and turquoise in crested couas. In flight, it is recognizable from its short rounded wings and long white-tipped tail.
Furthermore, this bird's third toe is reversible, which helps it have a strong grip when hopping around high branches in the canopy. Although they are common birds in their range, they can be hard to see in the wild since their greenish-gray coloration tends to blend with shadows and act as camouflage. Males and females are similar physically.


Juveniles
Juveniles are comparable to adult crested couas however, they are paler in colour, have a shorter head crest and do not have the characteristic bright blue and turquoise ring around the eye.


Chicks
Young chicks are altricial, which means they are naked at birth and need full parental attention for their early development. During that time, they have unique red and white "bullseyes" inside their mouth, supposedly used to indicate to their parents where to put the food. This characteristic is thought to be particularly useful at darker hours of the day. They also have a dark skin and a reddish bill at that stage.


Taxonomy
The crested coua is one of nine living bird species in the subfamily Couinae, which only has one genus, the Coua. These birds are part of the order Cuculiformes, which includes Cuckoos and Roadrunners. Couas are a monophyletic group of endemic birds of Madagascar.
Three of the coua species are arboreal (Blue Coua (C. caerulea), Crested Coua (C. cristata) and Verreaux's Coua (C. verreauxi)), and six species are ground dwelling (Coquerel's Coua (C. coquereli), Running Coua (C. cursor), Giant Coua (C. gigas), Red-fronted Coua (C. reynaudii), Red-capped Coua (C. ruficeps) and Red-breasted Coua (C. serriana)).


Subspecies
The crested coua has four subspecies:

Coua cristata cristata
Coua cristata dumonti
Coua cristata pyropyga
Coua cristata maxima


Distribution and habitat
Crested couas are endemic to Madagascar and are the most widespread of all couas. Although their population numbers are unknown, their range is thought to be 562,000 km2 along the coast of the island. They are found at altitudes as high as 900m above sea level, but are most commonly seen around altitudes of 700m.
Despite being mostly found in littoral and deciduous forests, their habitats also consist open areas like savannas and brushlands. They will occasionally be found in mangroves and palms as well. Additionally, they usually avoid deciduous shrublands, selecting mainly for mosaics of forests and croplands. As arboreal species, they tend to use forest layers above five meters and they nest, on average, around nine meters from the ground.


Threats
Although this species is marked as least concerned due to their stable populations, crested coua's numbers are believed to be declining due to habitat loss, degradation and hunting pressures.
It is mostly hunted by humans for meat, but its main natural predators are fossas, hawks and eagles.


Behaviour
Despite being capable of flight, crested couas are weak flyers, and often bounce continuously between branches in tree canopies. Keeping balance with their long tail and grasping branches with their reversible toe, they are able to move with speed and agility. Secretive, they tend to stay in groups of three to five individuals outside of breeding season. They do not tend to flock, although they sometimes join mixed-species flocks.


Vocalization
They are highly vocal birds and have several different calls that we can hear in the wild. The most commons are "laser gun calls", which are a series of piercing sounds, and doubled, wooden "touk-touk" sounds. It is also possible to hear calls such as "koa-koa-koa" and "guay-guay-guay-guay-gwuck". Its call can sometimes serve as an indicator of predator presence in the environment.
Couas, like many birds, tend to be more vocal around sunset, often creating mixed chorus with other species. They also raise their head crest while calling.


Reproduction
Breeding crested couas are usually found in monogamous pairs. Unlike some other species of cuckoos, these birds do not take part in brood parasitism, but rather, both male and female co-parent the chicks. The pair builds their nest themselves out of twigs and hide it in trees or bushes to avoid detection by predators. Two single white eggs are laid and incubated by both parents. The chicks are born altricial with both parents equally helping in raising the young until they are about two weeks old, when they leave the nest. Couas have low fecundity, but they have a high adult longevity (up to 15 years in captivity), which is why they can afford to have a smaller brood size. Additionally, because of their short young-rearing period, it is possible for them to have several clutches in one breeding season, which is from September to March.


Diet
They have an omnivorous diet consisting of arthropods, fruits, berries, snails, chameleons, leaves, seeds and others. They essentially feed on everything size appropriate that they find in their environment.


Gallery


References


External links
BirdLife Species Factsheet Archived 2007-09-29 at the Wayback Machine
https://www.xeno-canto.org/species/Coua-cristata (for songs, calls and distribution map).
https://whileatthezoo.com/tag/northern-crested-coua/ (to see the "bullseye" markings inside chick's mouth).

The '''crested coua''' ('''''Coua cristata''''') is a common medium-sized bird member of the cuckoo family, [[Cuckoo|Cuculidae]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|title=ITIS - Report: Coua cristata|url=https://www.itis.gov/servlet/SingleRpt/SingleRpt?search_topic=TSN&search_value=554744#null|access-date=2021-11-11|website=itis.gov}}</ref> It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to Madagascar,<ref name=":3">{{Cite journal|last=Chiatante|first=Gianpasquale|title=Spatial distribution of an assemblage of an endemic genus of birds: an example from Madagascar|journal=African Journal of Ecology|year=2021|volume=60|pages=13–26|doi=10.1111/aje.12917|s2cid=239682915|issn=1365-2028|doi-access=free}}</ref> mainly found in the coastal regions of the island.<ref name=":11">{{Cite web|title=Coua cristata (Crested Coua) - Avibase|url=https://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?avibaseid=FCAB43238B5B65A1|access-date=2021-11-12|website=avibase.bsc-eoc.org}}</ref> The crested coua is a weak flyer, so it will often be observed hopping from branch to branch in high canopies.<ref name=":9">{{Cite journal|last1=URANO|first1=EIICHIRO|last2=YAMAGISHI|first2=SATOSHI|last3=ANDRIANARIMISA|first3=ARISTIDE|last4=ANDRIATSARAFARA|first4=SOLOFO|date=2008-04-03|title=Different habitat use among three sympatric species of couas Coua cristata, C. coquereli and C. ruficeps in western Madagascar|url=https://onlinelibrary-wiley-com.proxy3.library.mcgill.ca/doi/abs/10.1111/j.1474-919X.1994.tb01125.x?sid=worldcat.org|journal=Ibis|volume=136|issue=4|pages=485–487|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919x.1994.tb01125.x|issn=0019-1019|url-access=subscription}}</ref><ref name=":4">{{Cite web|title=Crested Coua - eBird|url=https://ebird.org/species/crecou1|access-date=2021-11-11|website=ebird.org}}</ref> It is distinguished mainly by its greenish-grey back, its prominent grey head crest, rufous-coloured breast, white abdomen and bright turquoise and blue patches of bare skin around the eyes.<ref name=":5">{{Cite journal|last1=Kirwan|first1=Guy M.|last2=Payne|first2=Robert B.|last3=del Hoyo|first3=Josep|last4=Collar|first4=Nigel|editor1-first=Shawn M|editor1-last=Billerman|editor2-first=Brooke K|editor2-last=Keeney|editor3-first=Thomas S|editor3-last=Schulenberg|date=2020-03-26|title=Crested Coua (Coua cristata)|url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/crecou1/cur/introduction|journal=Birds of the World|doi=10.2173/bow.crecou1.01.1|s2cid=241512973|url-access=subscription}}</ref>  

== Description ==

=== Adults ===
The adult crested coua is a medium-sized bird, averaging between 12 and 17 inches (30 to 44 cm) in length.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Crested Couas {{!}} Beauty of Birds|url=https://www.beautyofbirds.com/crestedcouas.html|access-date=2021-11-11|website=beautyofbirds.com}}</ref><ref name=":6">{{Cite web|title=Crested Coua|url=https://www.rosamondgiffordzoo.org/|access-date=2021-11-11|website=Rosamond Gifford Zoo}}</ref> It has a greenish-gray [[plumage]] on the back, a white abdomen, rufous-colored chest and brown eyes. Its long tail is purplish-blue with white tips and its bill and beak are black. Its defining characteristics are its prominent grey [[Crest (feathers)|head crest]] and, as goes for all [[coua]]s, its colourful patches of bare skin around the eyes, which is blue and turquoise in crested couas. In flight, it is recognizable from its short rounded wings and long white-tipped tail.

Furthermore, this bird's third toe is reversible, which helps it have a strong grip when hopping around high branches in the canopy. Although they are common birds in their range, they can be hard to see in the wild since their greenish-gray coloration tends to blend with shadows and act as camouflage.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Nathan|date=2013-02-11|title=The Bird with a Bullseye - Crested Coua|url=http://www.wild-facts.com/2013/crested-coua/|access-date=2021-11-11|website=Animal Facts For Kids {{!}} Wild Facts}}</ref> Males and females are similar physically.

=== Juveniles ===
Juveniles are comparable to adult crested couas however, they are paler in colour, have a shorter head crest and do not have the characteristic bright blue and turquoise ring around the eye.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Payne|first=Robert B.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FX3dzcG-WoMC&dq=crested+coua+%22juvenile%22&pg=PA632|title=The Cuckoos|date=2005-07-14|publisher=OUP Oxford|isbn=978-0-19-151355-8}}</ref>

=== Chicks ===
Young chicks are [[Altriciality|altricial]], which means they are naked at birth and need full parental attention for their early development.<ref name=":8">{{Cite web|last=CHOUTEAU|first=Philippe|date=2003|others=École Normale Supérieure Laboratoire d'Écologie|title=Breeding and territoriality of three ground coua species (Aves, genus Coua, Cuculidae) in the dry forest of western Madagascar|url=http://hdl.handle.net/2042/55549|access-date=2021-11-11|website=hdl.handle.net|hdl=2042/55549|language=fr}}</ref> During that time, they have unique red and white "bullseyes" inside their mouth, supposedly used to indicate to their parents where to put the food. This characteristic is thought to be particularly useful at darker hours of the day.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":6"/> They also have a dark skin and a reddish bill at that stage.

== Taxonomy ==
The crested coua is one of nine living bird species in the subfamily Couinae, which only has one genus, the [[Coua]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Urano|first1=Eiichiro|last2=Yamagishi|first2=Satoshi|last3=Andrianarimisa|first3=Aristide|last4=Andriatsarafara|first4=Solofo|date=1994|title=Different habitat use among three sympatric species of couas Coua cristata, C. coquereli and C. ruficeps in western Madagascar|url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/j.1474-919X.1994.tb01125.x|journal=Ibis|volume=136|issue=4|pages=485–487|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1994.tb01125.x|issn=1474-919X|url-access=subscription}}</ref> These birds are part of the order [[Cuckoo|Cuculiformes]], which includes [[Cuckoo]]s and [[Roadrunner]]s.<ref name=":2" /> Couas are a [[Monophyly|monophyletic]] group of endemic birds of Madagascar.<ref name=":3" />

Three of the coua species are arboreal ([[Blue coua|Blue Coua]] (''C''. ''caerulea)'', Crested Coua (''C''. ''cristata)'' and [[Verreaux's coua|Verreaux's Coua]] (''C''. ''verreauxi)''), and six species are ground dwelling ([[Coquerel's coua|Coquerel's Coua]] (''C''. ''coquereli)'', [[Running coua|Running Coua]] (''C''. ''cursor)'', [[Giant coua|Giant Coua]] (''C''. ''gigas)'', [[Red-fronted coua|Red-fronted Coua]] (''C''. ''reynaudii)'', [[Red-capped coua|Red-capped Coua]] (''C''. ''ruficeps'') and [[Red-breasted coua|Red-breasted Coua]] (''C''. ''serriana)'').<ref name=":3" />

=== Subspecies ===
The crested coua has four subspecies:<ref name=":2" />

* ''Coua cristata cristata''
* ''Coua cristata dumonti''
* ''Coua cristata pyropyga'' 
* ''Coua cristata maxima''

== Distribution and habitat ==
Crested couas are endemic to Madagascar and are the most widespread of all couas. Although their population numbers are unknown, their range is thought to be 562,000 km<sup>2</sup> along the coast of the island. They are found at altitudes as high as 900m above sea level, but are most commonly seen around altitudes of 700m.

Despite being mostly found in [[Littoral forest|littoral]] and [[deciduous forests]], their habitats also consist open areas like savannas and brushlands. They will occasionally be found in mangroves and palms as well. Additionally, they usually avoid deciduous shrublands, selecting mainly for mosaics of forests and croplands.<ref name=":3" /> As arboreal species, they tend to use forest layers above five meters and they nest, on average, around nine meters from the ground.<ref name=":9" />

== Threats ==
Although this species is marked as [[Least-concern species|least concerned]] due to their stable populations,<ref>{{Cite journal|last=International)|first=BirdLife International (BirdLife|date=2016-10-01|title=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species: Coua cristata|url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/en|journal=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species}}</ref> crested coua's numbers are believed to be declining due to habitat loss, degradation and hunting pressures.<ref name=":3" />

It is mostly hunted by humans for meat, but its main natural predators are [[Fossa (animal)|fossas]], [[hawk]]s and [[eagle]]s.<ref name=":10">{{Cite journal|last1=Seiler|first1=Melanie|last2=Schwitzer|first2=Christoph|last3=Holderied|first3=Marc|date=2013-10-01|title=Anti-predator behaviour of Sahamalaza sportive lemurs, Lepilemur sahamalazensis, at diurnal sleeping sites|url=https://brill.com/view/journals/ctoz/82/3/article-p131_3.xml|journal=Contributions to Zoology|volume=82|issue=3|pages=131–S1|doi=10.1163/18759866-08203003|issn=1875-9866|doi-access=free}}</ref>

== Behaviour ==
Despite being capable of flight, crested couas are weak flyers, and often bounce continuously between branches in tree canopies. Keeping balance with their long tail and grasping branches with their reversible toe, they are able to move with speed and agility.<ref>{{Cite web|title=WCS Wild View: No Trick, Crested Coua Is A Beautiful Treat|url=https://blog.wcs.org/photo/2017/10/13/crested-coua-no-trick-natures-most-beautiful-treat-bronx-zoo-bird/|access-date=2021-11-11|website=blog.wcs.org}}</ref> Secretive, they tend to stay in groups of three to five individuals outside of breeding season. They do not tend to [[Flocking (behavior)|flock]], although they sometimes join mixed-species flocks.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Crested Coua {{!}} North Carolina Zoo|url=https://www.nczoo.org/wildlife/animals/crested-coua|access-date=2021-11-11|website=nczoo.org}}</ref>

=== Vocalization ===
They are highly vocal birds and have several different calls that we can hear in the wild.<ref name=":4" /> The most commons are "laser gun calls", which are a series of piercing sounds, and doubled, wooden "touk-touk" sounds.<ref>{{Cite book|last1=Behrens|first1=Ken|url=https://www.degruyter.com/document/doi/10.1515/9781400880676-035/html|title=CUCKOOS|last2=Barnes|first2=Keith|date=2016-10-25|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-1-4008-8067-6|doi=10.1515/9781400880676-035}}</ref> It is also possible to hear calls such as "koa-koa-koa" and "guay-guay-guay-guay-gwuck".<ref name=":6" /><ref name=":12" /> Its call can sometimes serve as an indicator of predator presence in the environment.<ref name=":10" />

Couas, like many birds, tend to be more vocal around sunset, often creating mixed chorus with other species.<ref name=":7">{{Cite web|title=Animals of Madagascar {{!}} Charles Paddock Zoo {{!}} Your Central Coast Zoo|url=https://charlespaddockzoo.org/animal/crested-coua/|access-date=2021-11-11|website=charlespaddockzoo.org}}</ref> They also raise their head crest while calling.<ref name=":5" />

=== Reproduction ===
Breeding crested couas are usually found in [[Monogamy in animals|monogamous pairs]].<ref name=":8" /> Unlike some other species of cuckoos, these birds do not take part in [[Brood parasite|brood parasitism]], but rather, both male and female co-parent the chicks.<ref name=":0" /> The pair builds their nest themselves out of twigs and hide it in trees or bushes to avoid detection by predators. Two single white eggs are laid and incubated by both parents.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Rearing Couas in Weltvogelpark Walsrode {{!}} Beauty of Birds|url=https://www.beautyofbirds.com/breedingcouas.html|access-date=2021-11-12|website=beautyofbirds.com|date=16 September 2021 }}</ref> The chicks are born [[Altriciality|altricial]] with both parents equally helping in raising the young until they are about two weeks old, when they leave the nest. Couas have low [[fecundity]], but they have a high adult [[longevity]] (up to 15 years in captivity), which is why they can afford to have a smaller [[Clutch (eggs)|brood size]]. Additionally, because of their short young-rearing period, it is possible for them to have several clutches in one breeding season, which is from September to March.<ref name=":12">{{Cite web|date=2019-12-28|title=The Crested Coua|url=https://www.madamagazine.com/en/der-spitzschopf-seidenkuckuck/|access-date=2021-11-12|website=MADAMAGAZINE}}</ref>

=== Diet ===
They have an omnivorous diet consisting of arthropods, fruits, berries, snails, chameleons, leaves, seeds and others. They essentially feed on everything size appropriate that they find in their environment.<ref name=":12" /><ref name=":5" />

zh.extract:
凤头马岛鹃（學名：Coua cristata），与科奎氏马岛鹃、红顶马岛鹃都属杜鵑科岛鹃屬，中等身型，身长大约44厘米，头冠绿灰色，蓝裸色眼睛，胸脯红褐色，嘴巴和腿部为黑色，腹部白色，紫蓝色尾翼，尾翼末端呈白色，长而尖，栖在枝头时常上下翘动。
凤头马岛鹃是马达加斯加特有的鸟类，主要生活在马达加斯加從海平面至海拔900公尺的森林、草原和丛林裡，喜欢干性环境，习惯上栖息在5米以上的高处，但有时也会在1-5米的高处活动，梳理羽毛、晒太阳、鸣叫。凤头马岛鹃主要以各种昆虫、水果、浆果、种子、蜗牛和蜥蜴为食。雌性凤头马岛鹃通常一次产下两个白色的蛋，鸟巢用细细的树枝做成。凤头马岛鹃在其整个栖息地范围为普遍常见的物种，在世界自然保护联盟濒危物种红色名录上被评为極少关注物种。
